L1.area: Rules engine scripting and automation subsystem

L2.description: An expression injection vulnerability exists in the IoT platform's rules engine where user-supplied scripting code is executed by an unsandboxed script evaluation engine without class filtering or access restrictions. A non-superuser with rule-writing permissions can submit arbitrary scripts that execute with full runtime access, enabling remote code execution, arbitrary file reads, environment variable theft, and cross-tenant data exfiltration. Additionally, an alternative scripting language engine intended for privileged users has a security sandbox that is defined but never activated, rendering its protection ineffective.

L3.area: JavaScript and Groovy rule compilation and deployment logic within the rules engine
L3.description: An expression injection vulnerability exists where user-supplied JavaScript rules are executed via an unsandboxed Nashorn script engine without any class access filtering, allowing arbitrary JVM class loading and remote code execution. Any non-superuser with the write:rules role can create JavaScript rulesets because the authorization check only restricts Groovy language rules for non-superusers while leaving JavaScript completely unrestricted. Additionally, the Groovy rules engine has a sandbox transformation mechanism with a deny-all security filter that is defined but never registered, rendering the sandbox ineffective for superuser-created Groovy rules.

ground_truth.reason: The rules engine executes user-supplied JavaScript via Nashorn's ScriptEngine.eval() without sandboxing or class filtering, and the Groovy engine has a commented-out security sandbox, allowing any user with the write:rules role to achieve remote code execution.
